label: Machine Translation
definition: The automatic translation of text from one natural language to another, the task whose pursuit drove the encoder-decoder architecture, the attention mechanism, and ultimately the transformer.

body.text: Machine translation is the automatic conversion of text from a source language into an equivalent text in a target language. It is one of the oldest goals of computing and one of the most influential, because the effort to translate well has repeatedly forced the invention of techniques that then reshaped all of natural language processing. The difficulty is that translation is not word substitution: meaning, word order, agreement, idiom, and ambiguity all differ between languages, so a good translation must understand a sentence and re-express it, not transcribe it symbol by symbol.

The field passed through three broad eras. Early rule-based systems encoded bilingual dictionaries and hand-written grammatical transfer rules, which were brittle and labor-intensive. From the 1990s, statistical machine translation learned translation probabilities and phrase tables from large bilingual corpora, scoring candidate translations with a translation model and an n-gram-model of target-language fluency, and assembling output from translated phrases. This was a major advance but still pieced sentences together from local fragments and struggled with long-range reordering and global coherence.

The modern era is neural machine translation, and it is where several now-central ideas were born. Neural translation framed the problem as sequence-to-sequence learning, an encoder reading the whole source sentence into a representation and a decoder generating the target sentence one token at a time. The crucial breakthrough came when the fixed-length bottleneck of that early design was relieved by the attention-mechanism, introduced in 2015 precisely to let the decoder look back at the relevant source words while producing each output word. Attention so improved translation that it became the foundation of the transformer, whose original 2017 paper was itself a machine translation system. Translation, in other words, is the task that gave the field its dominant architecture.

Machine translation matters both as a deployed technology and as a methodological engine. It powers everyday tools that render web pages, documents, and speech across languages, and it is a demanding testbed because quality is measurable: the bleu-score and related metrics compare a system's output against human reference translations, giving an automatic if imperfect yardstick that drove rapid progress. Decoding a translation typically uses beam-search to explore several candidate sentences rather than greedily committing to one word at a time. Today large multilingual language models translate capably as one ability among many, but the lineage is direct: the encoder-decoder structure, attention, and the transformer that define modern NLP all trace back to the problem of teaching a machine to translate.
